title: DS Intro
content:
Data Science Introduction

The Power of Data

Data Science is an interdisciplinary field that uses scientific methods, processes, algorithms, and systems to extract knowledge and insights from noisy, structured, and unstructured data.



  • Combines statistics, mathematics, and computer science.
  • Used for predictive modeling, pattern recognition, and decision making.
  • Empowers businesses to optimize operations and personalize user experiences.

title: DS Lifecycle
content:
Data Science Lifecycle

Steps to Insights

A typical data science project follows a structured process to ensure reliable results.



  • Data Collection: Gathering data from various sources (SQL, APIs, Web Scraping).
  • Data Cleaning: Removing duplicates, handling missing values, and fixing inconsistencies.
  • EDA: Understanding patterns and relationships through visualization.
  • Modeling: Applying machine learning algorithms to predict or classify.
  • Deployment: Integrating the model into a production environment.

title: Python for DS
content:
Python for Data Science

Why Python?

Python is the most popular language for Data Science due to its simplicity and extensive ecosystem of libraries.

Checking Python Environment
import sys
import platform

print(f"Python Version: {platform.python_version()}")
print(f"Executable: {sys.executable}")

title: NumPy Basics
content:
NumPy: Numerical Python

Fast Array Operations

NumPy is the fundamental package for scientific computing in Python. It provides a powerful N-dimensional array object.

NumPy Array Creation
import numpy as np

# Create a 2D array
arr = np.array([[1, 2, 3], [4, 5, 6]])

print("Array:\n", arr)
print("Shape:", arr.shape)
print("Mean:", np.mean(arr))

title: Pandas Intro
content:
Pandas: Data Analysis

DataFrames and Series

Pandas provides high-performance, easy-to-use data structures and data analysis tools.

Creating a DataFrame
import pandas as pd

data = {
    'Name': ['Alice', 'Bob', 'Charlie'],
    'Age': [25, 30, 35],
    'City': ['New York', 'London', 'Paris']
}

df = pd.DataFrame(data)
print("DataFrame:\n", df)
print("\nAverage Age:", df['Age'].mean())

title: Data Viz
content:
Data Visualization

Visualizing with Matplotlib

Visualization is key to understanding complex data patterns and communicating insights.

Plotting with Matplotlib
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

x = np.linspace(0, 10, 100)
y = np.sin(x)

plt.plot(x, y)
plt.title("Sine Wave")
plt.xlabel("X axis")
plt.ylabel("Y axis")
print("Chart rendered successfully (in a real environment)")

title: EDA Basics
content:
Exploratory Data Analysis

Uncovering Hidden Patterns

EDA is the process of performing initial investigations on data to discover patterns, spot anomalies, and test hypotheses.



  • Checking for null values (`df.isnull().sum()`).
  • Descriptive statistics (`df.describe()`).
  • Correlation analysis (`df.corr()`).
  • Visualizing distributions (Histograms, Boxplots).

title: ML Intro
content:
Machine Learning Intro

Teaching Machines to Learn

Machine Learning is a subset of AI that provides systems the ability to automatically learn and improve from experience without being explicitly programmed.



  • Supervised Learning: Learning from labeled data (e.g., Regression, Classification).
  • Unsupervised Learning: Finding patterns in unlabeled data (e.g., Clustering).
  • Reinforcement Learning: Learning through trial and error.

title: Scikit-Learn
content:
Scikit-Learn Overview

Machine Learning in Python

Scikit-learn is the most popular library for machine learning in Python, providing simple and efficient tools for predictive data analysis.

Linear Regression with Scikit-learn
from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression
import numpy as np

# Sample data
X = np.array([[1], [2], [3], [4], [5]])
y = np.array([2, 4, 5, 4, 5])

model = LinearRegression()
model.fit(X, y)

prediction = model.predict([[6]])
print(f"Prediction for X=6: {prediction[0]:.2f}")

title: Preprocessing
content:
Data Preprocessing

Preparing Data for ML

Raw data is often messy and needs to be transformed before it can be used by machine learning models.



  • Feature Scaling: Standardizing or normalizing numerical features (e.g., StandardScaler).
  • Encoding: Converting categorical data into numerical format (e.g., One-Hot Encoding).
  • Handling Imbalance: Using techniques like SMOTE or undersampling for skewed datasets.
Standard Scaling with Scikit-learn
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
import numpy as np

data = np.array([[1, 2], [3, 4], [5, 6]])
scaler = StandardScaler()

scaled_data = scaler.fit_transform(data)
print("Scaled Data:\n", scaled_data)

title: Feature Eng.
content:
Feature Engineering

Creating Better Features

Feature engineering is the process of using domain knowledge to extract features from raw data that help machine learning algorithms work better.



  • Interaction Features: Combining two or more features.
  • Polynomial Features: Creating non-linear relationships.
  • Time-based Features: Extracting hour, day, or month from timestamps.
  • Binning: Grouping continuous values into discrete intervals.

title: Adv. Cleaning
content:
Advanced Data Cleaning

Beyond Missing Values

High-quality models require high-quality data. Advanced cleaning involves handling outliers and complex noise.



  • Outlier Detection: Using Z-score or IQR (Interquartile Range) to find anomalies.
  • Imputation: Using IterativeImputer or KNNImputer for complex missing patterns.
  • Data Validation: Ensuring data types and ranges are consistent.
Handling Outliers (IQR Method)
import numpy as np

data = np.array([10, 12, 12, 13, 12, 11, 14, 100]) # 100 is an outlier

q1, q3 = np.percentile(data, [25, 75])
iqr = q3 - q1
lower_bound = q1 - (1.5 * iqr)
upper_bound = q3 + (1.5 * iqr)

clean_data = data[(data >= lower_bound) & (data <= upper_bound)]
print("Clean Data:", clean_data)

title: Model Eval.
content:
Model Evaluation

Measuring Success

Evaluating your model is crucial to understand how it will perform on unseen data.



  • Classification Metrics: Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score, ROC-AUC.
  • Regression Metrics: Mean Absolute Error (MAE), Mean Squared Error (MSE), R-squared.
  • Cross-Validation: Dividing data into multiple folds to ensure robustness.
Cross-Validation Example
from sklearn.model_selection import cross_val_score
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
import numpy as np

# Dummy data
X = np.random.rand(100, 5)
y = np.random.randint(0, 2, 100)

clf = RandomForestClassifier()
scores = cross_val_score(clf, X, y, cv=5)
print(f"Average Accuracy: {scores.mean():.2f}")

title: Classification
content:
Classification Algorithms

Predicting Categories

Classification is used when the output variable is a category, such as 'Spam' or 'Not Spam'.



  • Logistic Regression: Predicts the probability of a binary outcome.
  • Decision Trees: Uses a tree-like structure of decisions.
  • Support Vector Machines (SVM): Finds the optimal hyperplane to separate classes.
  • K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN): Classifies based on the majority class of closest neighbors.

title: Adv. Regression
content:
Advanced Regression

Beyond Simple Lines

Regression can handle complex relationships and prevent overfitting using regularization.



  • Polynomial Regression: Models non-linear relationships by adding powers of features.
  • Ridge Regression (L2): Adds a penalty to prevent large coefficients.
  • Lasso Regression (L1): Can set some coefficients to zero, performing feature selection.
Ridge Regression Example
from sklearn.linear_model import Ridge
import numpy as np

X = np.array([[1], [2], [3]])
y = np.array([1, 2, 3])

model = Ridge(alpha=1.0)
model.fit(X, y)
print("Coefficient:", model.coef_)

title: Clustering
content:
Clustering (Unsupervised)

Finding Hidden Groups

Clustering is the process of grouping similar data points together without predefined labels.



  • K-Means: Partitions data into K clusters by minimizing variance within clusters.
  • Hierarchical Clustering: Builds a tree of clusters (Dendrogram).
  • DBSCAN: Finds clusters based on density, good for irregular shapes.
K-Means Clustering
from sklearn.cluster import KMeans
import numpy as np

X = np.array([[1, 2], [1, 4], [1, 0], [10, 2], [10, 4], [10, 0]])
kmeans = KMeans(n_clusters=2, n_init='auto').fit(X)

print("Cluster Centers:\n", kmeans.cluster_centers_)
print("Labels:", kmeans.labels_)

title: Hyperparameter Tuning
content:
Hyperparameter Tuning

Optimization for Performance

Hyperparameters are settings that are not learned from data but set before training.



  • Grid Search: Exhaustively tries all combinations of parameters.
  • Random Search: Randomly samples parameter combinations.
  • Bayesian Optimization: Uses probability to find the best parameters faster.
GridSearchCV Example
from sklearn.model_selection import GridSearchCV
from sklearn.svm import SVC

param_grid = {'C': [0.1, 1, 10], 'kernel': ['linear', 'rbf']}
grid = GridSearchCV(SVC(), param_grid, refit=True)
# grid.fit(X, y) 
print("Grid search configured with parameters:", param_grid)

title: DS Summary
content:
Data Science Summary 📊

Mastering the Data

You've completed the basic Data Science curriculum! You now understand how to handle data, visualize it, and build basic predictive models.

  • Core Libraries: NumPy, Pandas, Scikit-learn.
  • Key Concepts: EDA, Data Lifecycle, ML Models.
  • Next Steps: Deep Learning, Natural Language Processing, or Big Data technologies!

The world of data is vast. Keep practicing with real-world datasets from Kaggle to sharpen your skills!
